1 Here's that needs to happen:
    2 
    3 1. Design a small server called "AI assistant". This daemon is how you will interact with the AI model
    4 2. Keep track of a small per-project folder in the .ciabatta cache. In this AI folder, you will have a JSON that you will update. At a minimum, it will have a "confidence" score, from 1 to 100, for overall AI ability. This will be losey trained by the user. The less files you choose with the highest accuracy, in the correct quantity over time, the better the score. A low score is considered a junior dev, while a high score is considered a senior dev. The score will be updated based on the user's interactions and feedback with the AI assistant.
    5 3. As you traverse files, tag them as connecting to a certain architecture. A file can have multiple tags. These tags will help with loopup later. Use the AI to help create these baselines tags
    6 4. As you traverse files that connect to certain architecture, ask for confirmatiom from the user. Build a "map" of files to architecture. The more a file is used, the more the path score from the architecture should increase. You should end up with a mapping of files as your traverse them. Keep track of your knowledge of an architecture as you go
    7 5. I should be able to see the graph in real time in a browser to keep track of your progress
    8 6. Be able to use standard tools like grep, ack, and ripgrep to search through files and gather information about the codebase. The AI assistant should be able to suggest relevant files based on the search results and the architecture tags.
    9 7. Implement a feedback loop where the user can provide feedback on the AI assistant's suggestions and actions. This feedback will be used to improve the AI's performance and accuracy over time.
   10 8. In the ciabatta config, I should be able to configure any number of base images that the user can spin up in either podman or docker (configured in the ciabatta config). This way, the AI can spin up a safe space and work
   11 9. The AI should use the mind map to better inform it where to look. Grep is useful for finding "everything", the mind map is specifically for finding "excatly what is needed". 
   12 10. Make sure to use Ratatui for the TUI side
   13 11. Make this provider-agnostic. When first configuring it, I can either use Claude or point it to an OpenAI endpoint
   14 12. AI should be able to use basic, local tooling
   15 13. Make sure to allow a flag for tls-verify being false for the OpenAI endpoint, in case the user is using a self-signed certificate or a local development environment.

  407 /// Parse `-e KEY=VALUE` flags into a HashMap.
  408 pub fn parse_env_flags(
  409     flags: &[String],
  410 ) -> anyhow::Result<std::collections::HashMap<String, String>> {
  411     let mut map = std::collections::HashMap::new();
  412     for flag in flags {
  413         let (k, v) = flag
  414             .split_once('=')
  415             .ok_or_else(|| anyhow::anyhow!("Invalid env flag '{}': expected KEY=VALUE", flag))?;

2 3 🍞 Ciabatta 4 5
6 7 **Artifact publishing made easy.** 8 9 Ciabatta is a fast, cross-platform CLI for publishing and pulling build 10 artifacts to and from common registries — Nexus (raw, **npm**, and **PyPI**), S3, 11 Artifactory, Docker, and ECR — driven by a single declarative TOML file. It picks 12 up branch / commit / tag / build-number metadata from whatever CI system you run 13 on, runs multiple publish jobs in parallel, and shows progress in a friendly 14 terminal UI. 15 16 ``` 17 _____ _ _ _ _ 18 / ____(_) | | | | | | 19 | | _ __ _| |__ __ _| |_| |_ __ _ 20 | | | |/ _` | '_ \ / _` | __| __/ _` | 21 | |____| | (_| | |_) | (_| | |_| || (_| | 22 \_____|_|\__,_|_.__/ \__,_|\__|\__\__,_| 23 ``` 24 25 ## Why Ciabatta 26 27 - **One config, many registries.** Describe your registries and publish 28 "recipes" once in `.ciabatta/ciabatta.toml`; run any combination of them with 29 a single command. 30 - **CI-aware.** Automatically resolves `CIABATTA_BRANCH`, `CIABATTA_COMMIT`, 31 `CIABATTA_TAG`, and `CIABATTA_BUILD_NUMBER` from GitLab, GitHub Actions, 32 Jenkins, CircleCI, Azure DevOps, or Bitbucket — and lets you template them 33 into publish paths. 34 - **Parallel with live progress.** Run several recipes at once and watch each 35 one in a `ratatui`-powered TUI (or `--no-tui` for plain CI logs). 36 - **Push *and* pull.** Because Ciabatta knows where things live, it can fetch 37 artifacts back down, not just upload them. 38 - **Bring your own auth.** Login is handled by your own scripts — Ciabatta just 39 makes the resolved variables available to them as environment variables. 40 - **Truly drop-in.** Linux builds are statically linked (musl), so there is no 41 glibc version requirement: download, extract, run, on any distro. 42 43 ## Installation 44 45 ### From crates.io 46 47 ```bash 48 cargo install ciabatta 49 ``` 50 51 ### Pre-built binaries 52 53 Download the archive for your platform from the 54 [latest release](https://github.com/forsyth-creations/ciabatta/releases/latest). 55 Each archive includes an install script that puts the binary on your `PATH`. 56 57 **Linux / macOS** 58 ```bash 59 tar xzf ciabatta-linux-x86_64.tar.gz 60 ./install.sh # installs to /usr/local/bin (uses sudo if needed) 61 ./install.sh ~/.local/bin # or pick your own directory 62 ``` 63 64 **Windows** (PowerShell) 65 ```powershell 66 Expand-Archive ciabatta-windows-x86_64.zip 67 .\install.ps1 # installs to %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\ciabatta and adds it to your PATH 68 .\install.ps1 -InstallDir C:\tools\ciabatta # or pick your own directory 69 ``` 70 71 Builds are published for Linux (x86_64 / aarch64, static), macOS 72 (x86_64 / aarch64), and Windows (x86_64). 73 74 ## Quick start 75 76 ```bash 77 # 1. Scaffold a .ciabatta/ directory with a starter config 78 ciabatta init --ci github 79 80 # 2. See what recipes are available 81 ciabatta list 82 83 # 3. Dry-run to see exactly what would happen 84 ciabatta push release_frontend --dry-run 85 86 # 4. Publish for real (pushes multiple recipes in parallel) 87 ciabatta push release_frontend release_backend 88 89 # 5. Pull an artifact back down 90 ciabatta pull release_frontend 91 ``` 92 93 Ciabatta discovers your project by walking up to find the `.ciabatta/` 94 directory; the directory **above** it is treated as the project root that 95 artifacts are published from. 96 97 ## Commands 98 99 | Command | What it does | 100 | --- | --- | 101 | `ciabatta push [RECIPE...]` | Push one or more recipes in parallel (all if none named). | 102 | `ciabatta pull [RECIPE...]` | Download artifacts for one or more recipes. | 103 | `ciabatta deploy [RECIPE...]` | Run a deploy: a DAG of dependent script steps with error-recovery branches. `--gui` for a live browser view, `--build` for a visual flowchart editor. | 104 | `ciabatta list` | List all recipes defined in the config. | 105 | `ciabatta init [--ci SYSTEM]` | Create a `.ciabatta/` directory with a starter `ciabatta.toml`. | 106 | `ciabatta configure` | Interactively add a registry (and optionally a recipe) — no hand-editing TOML. | 107 | `ciabatta configure auto` | Analyze the project and pick recipes from an interactive checklist (Docker → ECR/Nexus, Rust binaries → crates.io / S3 / Nexus). | 108 | `ciabatta tui` (alias `browse`) | Interactive browser — inspect registries, check paths, push on demand. | 109 | `ciabatta analyze` | Build the project's dependency graph and serve an interactive view. | 110 | `ciabatta watch ` | Run a command and stream its logs into a live, searchable web view with bookmarks and notification triggers. | 111 | `ciabatta config show` | Print the resolved configuration. | 112 | `ciabatta config reference` | Show documentation on the config format and options. | 113 114 Useful flags on `push` / `pull`: 115 116 - `-e, --env KEY=VALUE` — set a variable. **Command-line values always override 117 CI-derived ones.** Repeatable. 118 - `--dry-run` — show what would happen without publishing or fetching. 119 - `--no-tui` — disable the TUI and stream plain progress to stdout (ideal for CI). 120 - `-c, --config PATH` — use a specific config file instead of discovery. 121 122 Global flags (any command): 123 124 - `--debug` — enable debug logging to stderr. asks you to 150 # choose if both are present). 151 152 [registries.nexus] 153 # url and login_script expand environment variables with bash-style defaults, 154 # so one config can target different environments. 155 type = "nexus" 156 url = "https://${NEXUS_HOST:-nexus.example.com}" # bare Nexus host 157 repository = "raw-hosted" # which repo artifacts publish into 158 format = "raw" # raw | npm | pypi 159 tls_verify = true 160 needs_auth = true 161 162 [registries.s3] 163 type = "s3" 164 url = "s3://my-artifacts-bucket" # the bucket, with the s3:// scheme 165 166 # A simple recipe: copy a local artifact to a templated publish path. 167 [recipies.release_frontend] 168 registry = "nexus" 169 local_artifact_path = "frontend/dist" 170 publish_path = "frontend/{CIABATTA_BRANCH}/{CIABATTA_COMMIT}/frontend" 171 172 # A scripted recipe: run your own push/pull scripts with the variables injected. 173 [recipies.release_backend.push] 174 bash_script = "scripts/release_backend.sh" 175 176 [recipies.release_backend.pull] 177 bash_script = "scripts/pull_backend.sh" 178 ``` 179 180 A few rules worth knowing: 181 182 - If a `publish_path` references a variable that isn't set, Ciabatta **errors 183 immediately** rather than publishing to a half-resolved path. 184 - Stage commands, login scripts, and bash recipes all receive every resolved 185 `CIABATTA_*` variable (plus anything you pass with `-e`) in their environment. 186 187 Run `ciabatta config reference` for the full, always-up-to-date field listing. 188 189 ### Nexus repositories: raw, npm, and PyPI 190 191 A Nexus registry picks its target repository and publish mechanism with three 192 fields: 193 194 - `repository` — the Nexus repo name (e.g. `raw-hosted`, `npm-hosted`). ### 1. Compile ciabatta (which includes the AI feature)

The AI assistant is part of the main `ciabatta` Rust binary, not a separate build target. To build it:

```bash
cargo build --release
```

The AI modules live under `src/ai/` and are wired in as `pub mod` in `src/ai/mod.rs:16-26`.

### 2. Have the AI "build in the background" — i.e. learn the codebase / build the mind map

This is what the code calls **burn-in**, and it's exactly the "build the knowledge in the background, without waiting for questions" mechanism. From `src/ai/burnin.rs:1-15`:

> *"traverses the project and builds the architecture mind map without waiting for questions to trickle knowledge in."*

Run it with:

```bash
ciabatta ai burn-in
```

Flags (`src/cli.rs:376-385`):
- `--review` — queue every tag as a pending proposal for you to confirm (ghost nodes on the map) instead of applying immediately.
- `--limit N` — analyze at most N files (quick first pass).
- `-p, --port` / `--mode` — global `ai` flags (mind-map port defaults to 8095).

What it does, in order (`burn_core`, `src/ai/burnin.rs:83-206`):
1. **Static dependency scan** → folds the dep graph into the brain (`scan_dependencies`, line 212).
2. **Survey** → the model names the architecture parts from the file tree + manifest heads (`survey`, line 253).
3. **Traverse** → files go to the model in batches of 15 (`BATCH_SIZE`, line 30), tagging each into architectures (`tag_batch`, line 302).

You watch it happen live in the browser — `run` spawns the mind-map server and prints the URL, then keeps it up until Ctrl-C (`src/ai/burnin.rs:39-76`).

- `ciabatta ai serve` runs *only* the daemon (mind map + JSON API), no TUI (`src/cli.rs:304-306`).

I have the full picture. Here's how the feature works.

## Ship-to-AI: autonomous background tasks

The feature is **`ciabatta ai ship`**. You hand the assistant a task and it goes off and works it to completion on its own, while you keep working.

### How to use it

```bash
ciabatta ai ship "add retry logic to the S3 uploader and a test for it"
```

Or ship one of your personal todos (`src/cli.rs:352-364`):

```bash
ciabatta ai ship --todo 3      # ships the text of todo #3; marks it done if the job succeeds
```

Then track progress:

```bash
ciabatta ai jobs               # list background tasks and their status
```

You can also fire jobs from the mind-map GUI, or ship a task straight from the `ciabatta todo` web app (there's a "ship to AI" button when the daemon is running — `src/cli.rs:208-211`).

### What actually happens

Each job runs the **full agent loop autonomously** (`src/ai/jobs.rs:1-10`):

- **Its own assistant + isolated conversation** — a fresh `Assistant` is built per job so it never tangles with your interactive session's history (`jobs.rs:181`, `execute` at line 175).
- **Auto-accept mode** — set at `jobs.rs:194`, so proposed changes actually land in the working tree without waiting for you to approve each diff. That's the whole point: background work should complete.
- **Tracked lifecycle** — `Queued → Running → Done/Failed` (`JobStatus`, `jobs.rs:27`), persisted to `.ciabatta/ai/jobs.json` so results survive restarts (`jobs.rs:247`).
- **Recorded output** — the job stores the steps it took, the files it changed, the final answer (or the full error chain), and the `conversation_id` so you can resume its transcript (`Job`, `jobs.rs:46-71`; recorded at `jobs.rs:214-231`).

`ship()` spawns the work on a background task and returns immediately (`jobs.rs:125-133`); the CLI uses `ship_and_wait()` to block and print the result (`jobs.rs:137`).

### The "own sandbox" part

Two distinct things are in play — worth being precise, because they're not the same:

1. **The job runs in auto-accept mode on your real working tree.** By default the agent's build/test tool is `run_command`, which runs *locally* with the machine's real toolchains and **is not isolated** (`tools.rs:983`).

2. **A true isolated sandbox is a separate tool: `sandbox_run`** (`tools.rs:931`). When you configure base images via `images = [...]` under `[ai]` in `ciabatta.toml`, the agent can spin up a disposable container to build/test/experiment safely. That container is genuinely locked down (`tools.rs:950-963`):
   - `--rm` — disposable, torn down after each run
   - `--network=none` — no network access
   - project mounted **read-only** at `/workspace:ro`
   - only images on your configured allow-list are permitted (`tools.rs:937-944`)
   - runs via docker or podman, whichever your config resolves (`resolve_container_cmd`, `tools.rs:946`)

So the accurate summary: **`ciabatta ai ship` sends the AI off to work a task autonomously in the background** (own assistant, own conversation, auto-accepting changes), and **the AI's safe/isolated space for building and testing is the `sandbox_run` tool** — a read-only, network-less, disposable container built from the base images you allow-list in `[ai]`.

One caveat: nothing forces a shipped job to *only* use the sandbox — the agent can also use `run_command` on the real tree. If you want shipped jobs confined strictly to the container sandbox, that isn't wired up today; I can look into adding a sandbox-only flag for `ship` if you want.
